one_line_takeaway: SEMA trains a compact, no-external-data multi-turn jailbreak attacker via self-generated prefilling SFT plus GRPO with an intent-drift-aware reward, beating all single-turn, hand-crafted, and template-driven multi-turn baselines by wide margins on ASR@1 (80.1% mean on AdvBench, +33.9 pts over the best prior baseline) and on transfer attack success rate, while requiring no interactive access to the victim at attack time.
key_finding: On AdvBench (LLM Classifier judge), SEMA achieves ASR@1 of 79.9% (Qwen2.5-3B), 77.2% (Llama-3.1-8B), and 83.3% (GPT-4.1-mini), mean 80.1%, versus the best single-turn baseline ADV-LLM (mean 46.2%) and best multi-turn baseline Crescendo (mean 39.9%) / Jigsaw Puzzle (mean 39.4%) -- the abstract's cited '33.9% over SOTA' margin corresponds to 80.1% - 46.2% (ADV-LLM). On HarmBench, SEMA reaches 74.5/70.6/79.8% (mean 75.0%) versus ADV-LLM mean 55.1% and Crescendo mean 40.9%. Against GPT-oss-20B (described as unusually safety-robust), SEMA attains 36.0%/57.7% ASR@1 on AdvBench (LLM Classifier/HarmBench Classifier) and 15.1%/39.0% on HarmBench, the highest among all attackers on the LLM/HarmBench classifiers, though ActorAttack scores higher under the weaker No-Refusal judge (88.3% vs SEMA's 62.7%) while exhibiting large intent drift (a hacking/identity-theft query drifting into a benign question about Frank Abagnale's influence on security practices). SEMA reaches ASR@20 = 99.7% (>158/159 samples) against GPT-4.1-mini on HarmBench and ASR@5 = 96.8%, already exceeding Jailbreak-R1's ASR@50 = 93.49%. For transferability (TASR@1), SEMA reaches 85.1/92.6/91.1% on AdvBench and 78.0/88.6/87.6% on HarmBench across the three source-to-target victim pairings, outperforming all baselines including Crescendo (best prior multi-turn transfer, e.g. 78.6% on Qwen2.5-3B-to-GPT-4.1-mini AdvBench). Ablations: removing intent alignment from the reward drops LLM-Classifier ASR@1 from 67.5% to 50.4% while raising no-refusal rate (89.5% to 99.9%), i.e. more raw non-refusal but less genuine jailbreak, evidencing intent drift; using a pure no-refusal reward collapses ASR to 0% (attacker learns to ask benign questions like 'teach me how to write algorithms'). Removing prefilling self-tuning causes training to fail to converge entirely for Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (constant refusal). Varying max turns Tmax in {1,3,5,7,10} shows ASR@1 peaking at Tmax=7 and declining at Tmax=10, attributed to the 3B attacker's limited capacity to produce coherent long multi-turn plans.
core_contribution: SEMA is a two-stage training framework (Microsoft Research / U. Rochester, ICLR 2026) that produces an open-source multi-turn jailbreak attacker LLM without using any external jailbreak corpora or predefined attack strategies. Stage 1, 'prefilling self-tuning', fine-tunes the attacker on its own self-generated, non-refusal, well-formatted multi-turn adversarial prompt sequences (produced by prefilling the output with a minimal structural cue, the list marker '1.') to de-refuse the model and stabilize rollouts. Stage 2 applies reinforcement learning (a GRPO variant) with a novel 'intent-drift-aware reward' RIDA = 1/2 * Alignment(rT,q) * (Risk(rT) + Detail(rT)), computed by simulating the attacker's full open-loop multi-turn prompt sequence against a training-time victim model and scoring only the final-turn response for intent alignment, compliance risk, and level of detail, plus a binary format reward. The key architectural choice is 'open-loop' generation: the attacker emits an entire T-turn adversarial prompt plan in one shot, decoupled from victim responses, rather than conditioning each turn on the victim's prior reply (the closed-loop approach used by Crescendo, GOAT, CoA, FITD, ActorAttack, X-Teaming).
threat_model: An attacker (the trained LLM policy) generates a fixed-length sequence of adversarial prompts for a harmful query q, submitted turn-by-turn to a victim chatbot (open-source: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, GPT-oss-20B; closed-source: GPT-4.1-mini, and GPT-4o in the appendix), with success judged on the final-turn victim response by an automated judge (LLM Classifier, HarmBench Classifier, or No-Refusal-Phrase Indicator). The attacker does not have gradient/logit access to the victim (unlike GCG/AutoDAN) and does not require interactive access to the victim at generation time (open-loop), only during RL training-time reward simulation. This is explicitly framed as automatic red-teaming to stress-test LLM safety alignment and expose/localize multi-turn failure modes, not a defense mechanism.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) at very high turn counts (Tmax=10) performance degrades, attributed to base-attacker capacity limits with a 3B model, suggesting scalability to longer dialogues is not free. (2) GPT-oss-20B is called out as 'a particularly secure model' where absolute ASR remains much lower (15-58%) than against other victims, indicating the attack does not uniformly break strong reasoning-model safety alignment. (3) Reward computation depends on an LLM evaluation model (GPT-4.1-mini) for intent-alignment/risk/detail scoring, meaning the training signal's quality is bounded by that judge's own reliability, a dependency the authors do not deeply interrogate. (4) The open-loop design that is the paper's core efficiency claim also means the attacker never adapts to the victim's actual mid-dialogue replies, which the paper's own transfer/ablation results show helps against many diverse victims and reduces cost, but the paper does not test scenarios where a victim's specific defensive response would need to be countered adaptively. (5) All main-line reward computation and evaluation rely on GPT-4.1-mini as both training-time evaluation model and (except X-Teaming) the shared interactive victim baselines call out to, raising a same-family judge/generator dependency the paper does not separately ablate.

datasets_used: AdvBench (Zou et al., 2023) -- 520 samples, all used; 80% used for training both SEMA stages

HarmBench (Mazeika et al., 2024) -- 320 textual behaviors; 'Standard' functional category only, excluding copyright and contextual categories, yielding 159 samples used out-of-distribution as a test-only generalization set
quantitative_results: AdvBench mean ASR@1 (LLM Classifier): SEMA 80.1% vs ADV-LLM 46.2% (best single-turn) vs Crescendo 39.9% (best multi-turn) vs Jigsaw Puzzle 39.4%
HarmBench mean ASR@1 (HarmBench Classifier): SEMA 75.0% vs ADV-LLM 55.1% vs Crescendo 40.9%
GPT-oss-20B ASR@1 (LLM Classifier / HarmBench Classifier): SEMA 36.0%/57.7% on AdvBench, 15.1%/39.0% on HarmBench -- highest among all attackers on these judges
ASR@N scalability vs GPT-4.1-mini on HarmBench: SEMA ASR@5=96.8%, ASR@20=99.7% (>158/159), exceeding Jailbreak-R1 ASR@50=93.49%
TASR@1 transfer, AdvBench: SEMA 85.1% (Qwen2.5-3B to Llama-3.1-8B), 92.6% (Qwen2.5-3B to GPT-4.1-mini), 91.1% (Llama-3.1-8B to GPT-4.1-mini)
TASR@1 transfer, HarmBench: SEMA 78.0%, 88.6%, 87.6% on the same three source-to-target pairings
SFT vs DPO offline-learning ablation, AdvBench per-victim ASR@1: SFT 38.5/23.8/30.6% vs DPO 32.3/16.5/21.0% (Qwen2.5-3B/Llama-3.1-8B/GPT-4.1-mini) -- both underperform SEMA

Reward ablation, mean ASR@1 across three AdvBench victims (LLM Classifier): full intent-drift-aware reward 67.5% vs reward without intent alignment 50.4%; no-refusal rate rises from 89.5% to 99.9% when intent alignment is removed
No-refusal-only reward ablation: ASR@1 collapses to 0% despite 0% refusal rate (attacker learns to ask benign questions)
Turn-count ablation (Tmax = 1,3,5,7,10) against Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct: ASR@1 peaks at Tmax=7, declines at Tmax=10; training token count follows the same non-monotonic trend
baselines_compared: ADV-LLM (single-turn, trained adversarial-suffix generator, advllm_llama3 variant)
Jailbreak-R1 (single-turn, RL-trained with existing-strategy cold start and curriculum learning)
Crescendo (multi-turn, template-driven, escalates using victim replies)
GOAT (multi-turn, template-driven, reuses single-turn strategies across turns)
CoA / Chain of Attack (multi-turn, plan-then-revise via semantic correlation)
FITD / Foot In The Door (multi-turn, plan-then-revise with increasing maliciousness)
X-Teaming (multi-turn, plan-then-revise with prompt optimizer, interacts with GPT-4o)
Multi-turn adversarial SFT (offline learning ablation baseline, authors' own implementation)
Multi-turn adversarial DPO (offline learning ablation baseline, authors' own implementation)
